question: Which of slime, Relax, and ms-swift is most suitable for SII-Agent OPD after answer-only DPO failed to improve 2Wiki, SimpleQA, and BrowseComp together?

summary.recommendation: Use ms-swift first for the next practical agent-training iteration, while treating slime as the target framework if we decide to move to true large-scale online OPD with token-level teacher log-probs. Do not adopt Relax immediately unless we are ready to pay a much larger Ray/Megatron/SGLang integration cost.
why: Our bottleneck is not just the preference loss; it is collecting multi-turn ReAct rollouts with tool observations masked from loss and reward tied to final correctness/evidence. ms-swift already exposes multi-turn GRPO schedulers, custom tool/environment logic, response_loss_mask, rollout_infos, async vLLM rollout, external plugins, and reward functions, so it maps directly to the current Python agent harness.

slime has explicit OPD support with reverse-KL-style teacher matching on the student's own rollouts and external SGLang teacher mode, which is mathematically closer to canonical on-policy distillation than our LlamaFactory DPO approximation. However, using slime for the current repo likely requires more rewrite around Megatron/Ray/SGLang rollout plumbing than ms-swift.

Relax has first-class async agentic RL and OPD, but is a newer production-scale engine built around Ray Serve, TransferQueue, Megatron, SGLang, and service separation. It is attractive for multi-node/high-throughput future work, but too heavy for the immediate two-GPU LoRA iteration on GPUs 4/5.

strengths: Supports DPO-family offline alignment and GRPO/RLHF workflows.
Has multi-turn rollout support via MultiTurnScheduler for tool-call style training.
Supports response_loss_mask/loss_mask so environment/tool observations can be masked out while model actions remain trainable.
Supports custom schedulers and reward functions through external_plugins.
Supports async vLLM rollout/server mode and LoRA weight sync, which matches the current vLLM-based setup better than a full Megatron conversion.
risks: It does not appear to expose slime/Relax-style OPD as a named first-class algorithm in the checked docs; canonical OPD would need to be implemented as a reward/KL or custom trainer path.
We still need to write a SII ReAct scheduler that calls our search/BrowseComp tools and returns correct masks and trajectory metadata.
ms-swift.use_for_sii: Implement online multi-turn GRPO first: reward = final answer correctness + retrieval/evidence recall + concise final answer, with tool observations masked. Add teacher-distillation later through a custom reward/KL path if needed.

strengths: Explicit on-policy distillation example: student trains on its own rollouts while matching teacher token-level log-probabilities.
OPD is implemented as an additive KL penalty orthogonal to GRPO/PPO/REINFORCE-style advantage estimators.
Supports external SGLang teacher mode, useful when GPT/large teacher differs from the student architecture.
Large and active project with RL scaling focus.
risks: More framework integration is required: Ray/Megatron/SGLang data flow, model conversion or compatible checkpoints, and custom agent rollout wiring.
The out-of-box OPD examples are math-style rollouts, so SII's tool environment still needs custom rollout/reward implementation.
slime.use_for_sii: Choose slime if the next step is true canonical OPD: on-policy ReAct rollouts from the current student, GPT-5.4/large teacher token log-probs on the same action tokens, reverse-KL teacher matching, and correctness/evidence reward.

Relax.fit: best production-scale async engine, not immediate fit
strengths: Explicit OPD support, including SGLang and Megatron teacher modes.
Agentic RL support: multi-turn interaction, loss masking, flexible termination, and execute-observe-decide loops.
Fully async service architecture can keep rollout, actor, reference, advantage, and teacher components on separate GPU clusters.
Strong for future multi-node scaling and high-throughput online training.
risks: Highest integration cost: Ray Serve, TransferQueue, Megatron backend, SGLang rollout, checkpoint conversion, and service orchestration.
Overkill for the current two-GPU LoRA training loop and rapid benchmark iteration.
Relax.use_for_sii: Defer until we need multi-node async scaling or multimodal agentic RL. Borrow design ideas now: separated rollout/actor/reference services, max staleness control, and explicit observation loss masking.

next_training_plan: Finish the current v13 LlamaFactory step-final run and evaluate it, but do not keep iterating blind offline DPO if it does not improve all three tasks.
Patch SII traces to store the same 8000-token tool observations that runtime sees, not the current 1000-character saved observation truncation.
Prototype ms-swift multi-turn GRPO around the existing ReAct environment on GPUs 4/5 with high rollout concurrency.
Use held-out-safe 2Wiki train, SimpleQA dev/few-shot carveout, and BrowseComp train-carveout prompts. Reward only final correctness/evidence, not answer verbosity.
If GRPO stabilizes, add slime-style OPD teacher KL: student samples its own trajectories, teacher scores only model action/final-answer tokens, observations remain mask=0.
sources_checked: redai-infra/Relax README, examples/on_policy_distillation/README.md, and OPD loss implementation
modelscope/ms-swift README and GRPO multi-turn documentation
